# Transcrição: Lesson 17 - How to Make Good Habits Automatic

- **Fonte:** Lesson 17 - How to Make Good Habits Automatic
- **Duração:** 03:21
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

So a few years ago PetSmart, the popular pet food store, implemented an interesting strategy that showcases a lot about the importance of environment design for habits and behavior change. And what they did was they had a special screen that popped up during the checkout process. So people would walk about the store, get the items that they wanted to buy, and then as they were checking out, they would swipe their credit card, and then they were presented with a prompt that said, would you like to donate to help save homeless animals? And at this point, people could either choose yes and then select the amount that they would like to donate, or they could choose no and continue with the rest of the purchase. And through this one single strategy, without any marketing campaign, without talking to people about it, PetSmart Charities was able to raise over $40 million in a single year. And what is so brilliant about this idea is that yes, it was a good call to action. Yes, they had pet lovers who were coming into their store and they presented them with something that they were interested in. But what they really did was they stepped into the flow of what somebody was already doing and presented them with a hot trigger at a time when they could act on it and when they were capable of performing that behavior. And so the idea here is how can you design your environment so that the triggers that you come across will remind you within the flow of your normal day. So let's give you some examples. A few years ago, I wanted to build a flossing habit along with brushing my teeth each day. And so I knew that the flow and the pattern of when I brush my teeth was I would brush and then I would put the brush back down into its holder. And right next to it, I placed a bowl filled with pre-made flossers that were right there for me to grab up and pick. So in the flow of my normal routine, I was able to just add this new behavior on and I've been able to stick to it every day since. Another example is say you need to go to the gym. Well, you have a couple different things that you could do here to optimize. The first is you could select a gym that is literally on your path to work or on the path of a normal place that you go to. Because then you'll be driving by the gym anyway. It makes sense to leave early or to you know stop there on your way home because it's already within the flow of your normal behavior pattern. Similarly, if you're trying to get ready to go to the gym and optimize what you want to do or you'd like to just remind yourself to go to the gym, tonight when you're thinking about, oh, I should go work out tomorrow, set out your gym bag, set out your water bottle, have your clothes ready, and then just set those in front of your bed or in front of the door before you leave so that as you walk out, it's already in the flow of your behavior. You're able to grab it and have another reminder of the habit that you want to perform within the context of your normal day. You're not trying to stretch outside of the bounds of what you already have been doing. So these ideas of designing your behavior and getting into the flow of your normal pattern help you stick with new habits over the long run by capitalizing on the environment that you already operate within and just shaping that slightly to fit with the goals that you have for yourself. So now I'd like you to click on the worksheet below this video and

## [03:04 - 03:21]

list out at least 10 examples of how you could step into the flow of your normal day and place a reminder or a hot trigger that you can act on at that time to get yourself to fall in line and stick with the habits that you're looking to perform. So go ahead and do that now and I'll see you in the next video.
